steps.explanation: Create a dispatch function that tracks the current index. It calls the current middleware, passing context and a function that advances the index and dispatches the next middleware.

steps.title: Verify Output
steps.explanation: Execution logs show the onion pattern: MW1 before, MW2 before, MW3 final, MW2 after, MW1 after. The context accumulates step1, step2, step3. Each middleware controls when the next one runs via next().

challenges.title: Promise All
challenges.description: Implement `promiseAll(promises)` like `Promise.all`: resolve ordered results when all resolve, reject immediately on first rejection. `promiseAll([])` resolves to `[]`.
challenges.hint: Track remaining count and store by index.

challenges.title: Gen Range
challenges.description: Write a generator function genRange(start, end) that yields numbers from start to end inclusive. Test by spreading into an array. Expected output: `1,2,3,4,5`.
challenges.hint: Use yield inside a for loop.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need a generator that lazily produces a range of numbers.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Use a for loop inside a generator function, yielding each number from start to end.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: Spreading the generator into an array gives [1,2,3,4,5], which joins to 1,2,3,4,5.

challenges.title: Binary Hunt
challenges.description: Write function binarySearch(arr, target) that returns the index of target in a sorted array, or -1 if not found. Test with [1,3,5,7,9,11] and target 7. Expected output: `3`.
challenges.hint: Maintain lo and hi pointers, check the midpoint, and narrow the search range.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need to efficiently find a target in a sorted array using binary search.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Maintain two pointers (lo, hi). Compute mid, compare with target, and narrow the range accordingly.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: 7 is at index 3 in the array, matching the expected output.

challenges.title: Topo Sort
challenges.description: Write function topoSort(graph) that performs a DFS-based topological sort on a directed acyclic graph. Iterate nodes in insertion order and visit neighbors left-to-right. Test with {a:["b","c"], b:["d"], c:["d"], d:[]}. Expected output: `a,c,b,d`.
challenges.hint: Use DFS with a visited set; prepend each node to the result after visiting all its neighbors.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need to order graph nodes so that each node comes before its dependencies.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Use DFS: for each node, visit all neighbors recursively, then prepend the node to the result list.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: Running the code prints a,c,b,d — a valid topological ordering.

challenges.title: Proxy Trap
challenges.description: Create a function createSafeObject(obj) that returns a Proxy which returns 0 for missing properties. Test accessing an existing property and a missing one. Expected output: `42` followed by `0`.
challenges.hint: Use a Proxy with a get trap that checks if the property exists.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need a Proxy that intercepts property access and provides defaults for missing properties.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Create a Proxy with a get trap. Check if the property exists in target; if not, return 0.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: safe.x prints 42 (exists), safe.y prints 0 (default for missing).

challenges.title: Trie Build
challenges.description: Implement a Trie class with insert(word) and search(word) methods. Test inserting "cat", "car", "card" and searching for "car" and "cab". Expected output: `true` followed by `false`.
challenges.hint: Each node is an object mapping characters to child nodes, with an isEnd flag.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need a prefix tree that supports efficient word insertion and exact word lookup.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Each node is an object of character-to-child mappings. Insert traverses/creates nodes; search traverses and checks isEnd.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: "car" was inserted so search returns true; "cab" was not, so it returns false.

challenges.title: Graph Walk
challenges.description: Write function bfs(graph, start) that returns nodes in BFS traversal order. Test with {A:["B","C"], B:["D"], C:["E"], D:[], E:[]} starting from "A". Expected output: `A,B,C,D,E`.
challenges.hint: Use a queue and a visited set. Process nodes level by level.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need to traverse a graph breadth-first, visiting all nodes reachable from the start.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Use a queue initialized with start. Dequeue a node, add to results, and enqueue unvisited neighbors.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: BFS visits A first, then its neighbors B and C, then D and E, giving A,B,C,D,E.

challenges.title: Rate Gate
challenges.description: Write function createRateLimiter(maxCalls, windowMs) that returns a function allowing at most maxCalls within a sliding time window. Call it 3 times synchronously with a limit of 2 — the third call exceeds the limit. Expected output: `true`, `true`, then `false`.
challenges.hint: Track call timestamps and remove expired ones before checking the limit.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need a function that enforces a maximum call rate within a sliding time window.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Store timestamps of each call. Remove expired timestamps, then check if the count is below maxCalls.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: First two calls succeed (true, true), third exceeds the limit (false).

challenges.title: Linked Cycle
challenges.description: Write function hasCycle(head) that detects a cycle in a linked list using Floyd's tortoise and hare algorithm. Test with a cyclic list. Expected output: `true`.
challenges.hint: Use two pointers: slow moves one step, fast moves two steps. If they meet, there is a cycle.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need to detect a cycle in a linked list without using extra space.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Use Floyd's algorithm: move slow pointer one step and fast pointer two steps. If they meet, a cycle exists.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: The list has node3 pointing back to node1, creating a cycle, so the output is true.

challenges.title: Tree Balance
challenges.description: Write function isBalanced(root) that checks if a binary tree is height-balanced (no subtree differs in height by more than 1). Expected output: `true`.
challenges.hint: Write a helper that returns height or -1 if unbalanced. Check from the bottom up.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need to check whether a binary tree is height-balanced using recursive height computation.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Compute height bottom-up. Return -1 if any subtree is unbalanced; otherwise return max child height + 1.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: The test tree has max height difference of 1, so it is balanced and prints true.

challenges.title: JSON Path
challenges.description: Write function jsonPath(obj, path) that retrieves a value from a nested object using a dot-notation path string. Test with {a:{b:{c:42}}} and path "a.b.c". Expected output: `42`.
challenges.hint: Split the path by dots and reduce through the object, accessing each key in turn.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need to navigate a nested object using a string path like 'a.b.c'.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Split the path on dots and use reduce to traverse each key in sequence.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: Traversing a -> b -> c yields 42, matching the expected output.

challenges.title: Retry Logic
challenges.description: Write function retry(fn, retries) that calls fn up to retries times, returning the first successful result. Test with a function that succeeds on the 3rd attempt. Expected output: `Success on attempt 3`.
challenges.hint: Loop up to retries times, catch exceptions, and rethrow if all attempts fail.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need a retry mechanism that catches errors and retries until success or exhaustion.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Loop from 1 to retries. Try calling fn, catch errors, and rethrow on the last attempt.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: The function fails twice and succeeds on attempt 3, printing Success on attempt 3.

challenges.title: Custom Map
challenges.description: Implement myMap(arr, fn) from scratch without using Array.prototype.map. Test by doubling [1,2,3]. Expected output: `2,4,6`.
challenges.hint: Loop through the array and apply fn to each element, pushing results into a new array.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need to reimplement the map function using a basic for loop.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: Iterate through the array, call fn with (element, index, array), and collect results.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: Doubling [1,2,3] gives [2,4,6], which joins to 2,4,6.

challenges.title: Custom Reduce
challenges.description: Implement myReduce(arr, fn, initial) from scratch without using Array.prototype.reduce. Test by summing [1,2,3,4]. Expected output: `10`.
challenges.hint: Start with initial value (or first element if undefined) and iterate through the array.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need to reimplement reduce from scratch, handling both with and without an initial value.

steps.title: Implement the solution
steps.explanation: If initial is undefined, use the first element. Then iterate from the appropriate start index, accumulating results.

steps.title: Verify the result

challenges.title: Iterator Make

challenges.title: DFS Path
challenges.description: Write function dfsPath(graph, start, end) that finds a path between two nodes using depth-first search. Test with {A:["B","C"], B:["D"], C:[], D:["E"], E:[]} from "A" to "E". Expected output: `A,B,D,E`.
challenges.hint: Use recursive DFS with a visited set. Build the path as you go and return it when the end node is found.

steps.title: Implement the solution
